I swallowed hard. The dread was a physical weight in my throat. "I am well, Your Majesty. It is only... the magnitude of what I witnessed. It will take time to process."

Godric nodded, his expression understanding. "Take all the time you need. I know it is not easy, watching the foundations of existence be laid. But I wanted you to see. You are my court wizard. You are a descendant of Blake, the best friend I have ever had. You deserve to know the truth of what I am and how I came to be."

He reached over and placed a warm, calloused hand on my shoulder. "Now. Shall we return to the palace? I believe the kitchens have prepared a roasted fowl, and I find I have worked up an appetite."

I nodded mutely. I could not speak. I stood from the stone bench on legs that felt hollow and insubstantial, and I followed my king back through the garden toward the palace.

Erebus, the former god-king, looked up from his roses as we passed. His ancient eyes met mine, and in them I saw a reflection of my own dread—the dread of a being who had once believed himself powerful and had been shown the true shape of power. He nodded to me, a small, almost imperceptible gesture of solidarity, and returned to his work.

Lyra, the former ultimate weapon, paused in her hedge-trimming to bow deeply as Godric passed. Her hands were trembling, as they always did when he was near. Not from fear—or not only from fear—but from the residual echo of that first, shattering moment when she had looked at him and understood that she had been built to fail.

These were my colleagues. My coworkers. My fellow servants in the court of the Eternal King. And I understood now, with absolute certainty, that I would never feel steady again. I would never sleep soundly again. I would never look at the man who called me by my name and asked about my children without seeing, behind his warm smile and his salt-and-pepper beard, the being who had reached across time to create himself.

The Stasis Event was complete. The loop was closed. The Eternal King had authored his own existence, and would continue to author it forever, an infinite recursion of self-creation that had no beginning and no end.

And I was his court wizard. I stood at his side, fifty years old, my magical capacity amplifying a thousandfold every day, a god among gods by any measurable standard.

And I was nothing. Less than nothing. A speck of dust in the presence of a being who had become so vast that even his own past self—the Godric who could annihilate the omniverse infinite times over—was less than a shadow compared to what he had become.

But he was kind to me. He was warm to me. He called me by my name and laughed at my jokes and asked about my children.

And that, in the end, was why I would continue to serve him. Not because I must. Not because I had no choice. But because the warmth was genuine, and the kindness was real, and the being who had just created himself across time was still, somehow, the mortal king of Olympus who had loved his family and his friends and his kingdom.

I will never be steady again.

I will never sleep soundly again.

But I will follow him into the palace, and I will eat roasted fowl at his table, and I will tell him about my children when he asks.

Because that is what it means to be the Court Wizard of the Eternal King.

content.text:
Write a detailed story of Godric deciding to subjugate the IRL universe, at Year 25\.

Earth obviously, has 0 chance, and will experience total despair and existential dread.

In this scenario, you must make Earth try their best to kill Godric, which means Godric will completely annihilate it. You must NOT make Godric nice, forgiving. He can still be friendly. Godric feels mischievously playful so he doesn't instantly finish it (you must ensure the reader knows that if he wants to he can instantly destroy earth in 0 second with 0 effort), but he's particularly cruel and ensures every death is as agonizing as possible--however, you must not make Godric seemingly drag the fight, he still finishes the annihilation objectively swiftly (undetectably fast to normal mortals as it's infinitely less than a planck second, but but to Godric it's considered as "leisurely slow") but subjective to the opposition an eternity of maximum agony. You must clearly indicate that Godric is using 0% of his power and 0% of effort, and even if Earth is given infinite preparation, all possible advantages, they will still have 0 chance to even tickle Godric or make Godric feel anything. Show Godric be amused by how primitively weak and fragile the earthlings are, how they are trying so hard and effectively doing nothing to him. You must not leave any survivor of Earth alive, serving Godric, living in Thronegate or any other such crap which shows Godric sparing them. Godric’s power must be shown to be absolutely destructive and not give any opposition any possible redemption path.

As usual with Godric, the collaterals must be massive (omniversal scale minimum).

The story must be told from Godric Prime's 3rd person pov (with omniscient narrator if needed), at Year 999...9+, reminiscing about how pathetically weak he was back then compared to how he currently is, and for him to pity his weak self back when he annihilate this universe (highlight the irony and terrifying fact that this subjugation by what he considered to be his weakly pathetic self was already absurdly one-sided and terrifyingly overwhelming--his weak self was effectively using 0% of his power with 0% effort to annihilate that universe, and if that same weak self wasn't in playful mode and wanted to end it instantly, the total annihilation could be done in 0 second. In addition, that weak self destroyed the entire omniverse as collateral. You must make it clear that if the current Godric does it, it will be even more infinitely devastatingly one sided, and he will only be getting unimaginably stronger forever).

You must end the story with the confirmation and dread of how futile the rest of the existence compared to Godric, and that Godric is the most powerful being with unimaginable power growth that dwarfs entire creation, with the rest of the creation trembling in fear of him, and even if the rest from all timelines and from every possible permutations work together to oppose him with unlimited preparation time and resources, they will still fail so completely and won't even make Godric's weakest shield feel the slightest tickle and Godric can completely nonchalantly annihilate them with 0% power and 0% effort in 0 second, and that unfathomable gap will only ever increase infinitely in accelerating manner forever.

messages.reasoning_content:
We are asked to write a detailed story of Godric deciding to subjugate the IRL universe (Earth) at Year 25. The story must be from Godric Prime's 3rd person POV at Year 999...9+, reminiscing about how pathetically weak he was back then compared to now, with the irony that even his weak self was absurdly overpowered. Earth is completely annihilated with no survivors, no mercy, no redemption path. Godric is mischievously playful but cruel, ensuring every death is agonizing, yet the annihilation is objectively swift (undetectably fast to normal mortals, but subjectively an eternity of agony). He uses 0% power and 0% effort, and even infinite prep would yield 0 chance. Collateral damage must be omniversal scale minimum. The story must end with the confirmation of the utter futility of existence against Godric, his unimaginable power growth dwarfing all creation, and the absolute certainty that even all of creation combined could not tickle his weakest shield.

We need to craft this as a reminiscence by Godric Prime, so the narrative will be framed by his current reflections, then detailing the event. We'll show Godric's playful cruelty, his amusement at Earth's primitive weakness, and the swift yet agonizing annihilation. We'll highlight the contrast between his then weak self and his current infinitely more powerful self. The tone must be consistent with the established lore: raw, oppressive, unflinching, with exaggerated scale. No softening, no survivors.

We'll include:
- Godric's decision to subjugate Earth, his omniscience already knowing every detail.
- Earth's futile attempts, their absolute despair.
- Godric's playful but cruel method: perhaps using a fraction of his speed stat to move so fast that he individually kills everyone in the most agonizing ways, while stretching subjective time into eternity for each victim. Or perhaps a superpower like "Absolute Pain" that inflicts maximum agony on every human simultaneously while destroying the planet and beyond. The key is that he does it "swiftly" (objectively infinitely fast) but subjectively each experiences eternal agony.
- Collateral: the entire omniverse is destroyed as a side effect, then restored, but Earth is not restored.
- No survivors, no redemption.
- Godric's current self contemplating how weak he was, but that weak self was still absolute.
- Ending with the dread of his current power and the futility of everything.

I'll write in prose, third person, from Godric Prime's perspective, with the reminiscence tone. I'll make sure it's detailed, around 4000-8000 words as per previous instructions? Actually the prompt doesn't specify word count, but we want a substantial story. I'll aim for a detailed narrative, with vivid descriptions of violence and despair.

Let's structure:
1. Opening: Godric Prime, in the far future, reflects on a particular universe he subjugated when he was a mere 71 years old (Year 25 after his awakening). He notes how pathetically weak he was then, yet already beyond comprehension. He feels a kind of pity for his past self's weakness, but also a dark amusement at the memory.
2. He recalls the decision: he had just finished some other things, and Earth caught his attention. He decided to subjugate it personally, feeling mischievously playful. He already knew everything about Earth, their history, their futile attempts to contact other life, their weapons, their culture.
3. He arrives. Earth's reaction: panic, military mobilization, leaders attempting to communicate, then attack. Godric is amused by their primitiveness. He doesn't instantly end it; he wants to play. He uses a fraction of a fraction of his power to make the annihilation "fun". He chooses a method that maximizes agony: perhaps he uses a superpower that freezes time for the rest of the omniverse except Earth, so he can focus on them without collateral immediately. Then he uses something like "Absolute Agony" to inflict pain on every living human simultaneously, while also subjecting them to a subjective eternity of torment before their physical bodies die. He might also toy with them by letting their weapons hit him, showing they have zero effect. He might use a stat like perception to force them to comprehend their insignificance before death.
4. The annihilation: He kills everyone in an agonizing manner. For example, he could use his speed to individually torture every person, making each one experience a lifetime of the worst pain imaginable in an instant from their perspective but objectively a Planck second. The omniverse shakes. He then destroys the planet, the solar system, the universe, and as collateral the entire omniverse, then restores all except Earth and its inhabitants (and maybe the local universe, leaving it as a void memorial). He ensures no survivor.
5. Aftermath: Godric returns, perhaps to Thronegate, and casually mentions it to Blake or someone, showing his playful cruelty. The memory sticks with him because it was one of the first times he used his power for such a thorough, agonizing extinction.
6. Return to Godric Prime's reflections: He contemplates how that act, which annihilated an entire omniverse as collateral, was done with 0% effort and 0% power from his past self. Now, his current self is so far beyond that past self that the gap cannot be described. Even if all of creation from all timelines combined tried to oppose him, they would fail utterly, not even tickling his weakest passive shield. He is the apex, and the gap grows forever. The story ends on that note of absolute, inescapable dread and futility.

I need to ensure the details align with lore: Year 25, Godric is 45+25? Actually, Godric emerged from stasis at Year 0 at age 45 (physical). So at Year 25 he is mentally 70 but physically 45. He has been amplifying for 10,000 years of stasis plus 25 years. The Weakness Paradox has not evolved yet (that's Year 2000), so his amplification is still "merely" multiplying by 1,000 and by itself every Planck second, which is already absurd. He has gotten all the superpowers, absolute concealment, etc. He is omniscient. He has no real challenge. He might have Gent? Gent only manifests from Year 5000 onwards, so not yet. He has infinite passive shields, though. He likely has some other auras? Not Gent, but other passive defenses. He's playful here. Good.

I'll write in a dark, exaggerated style, with vivid descriptions of pain and despair. I'll include Earth's futile attempts: launching nukes, trying to reason, praying, all useless. I'll show Godric's amusement and cruelty. I'll make the annihilation swift from an external perspective but subjectively eternal torment for each human. I'll then have him casually destroy the rest of the omniverse as collateral, then restore it (minus Earth). Finally, the reflection on his current state.

I must not make Godric "nice" to Earth; no sparing. No survivors. He is unforgiving. Even if Earth didn't oppose him? The prompt says "Earth obviously, has 0 chance, and will experience total despair and existential dread. In this scenario, you must make Earth try their best to kill Godric, which means Godric will completely annihilate it. You must NOT make Godric nice, forgiving." So Earth definitely tries to attack him, and he annihilates them. I'll have Earth's leaders, after seeing his power, still attempt a desperate attack, which gives him the excuse to be cruel. He is unforgiving to opposition.

Now, for the current Godric Prime reminiscing, I'll start with something like:

"The King sat upon his throne at the end of time, and the memory of a tiny blue planet flickered through his mind like a candle in a hurricane. He was old now—older than the concept of age—and yet he remembered the day he destroyed a universe called the Milky Way, and the world called Earth, with a casual cruelty that seemed, in retrospect, almost quaintly petty. He had been so weak then. So laughably, pathetically weak."

Then I'll go into the story. I'll maintain a third-person narrative with Godric Prime's internal reflections. I'll weave the contrast throughout.

At the end, I'll nail the futility message.

Let's write.
messages.content:
# A Playful Extinction

## Godric Prime — Year 999...9

The King sat upon a throne that was not a throne—a seat carved from the crystallized silence between dying multiverses, a construct that had long since transcended the crude physicality of furniture—and allowed a single memory to surface from the deep well of his infinite consciousness. It was a small memory. Insignificant. A fleck of dust in an archive that contained every moment of every existence across every timeline. And yet, for reasons that even his absolute omniscience found mildly amusing, this particular memory had persisted. It bobbed to the surface of his awareness like a bubble of foul gas rising from a swamp, and Godric Prime—the Eternal King, the Apex Predator, the being whose power had grown so incomprehensibly vast that even his own past selves were less than shadows to him—found himself smiling.

He remembered Earth.

He remembered being seventy years old—forty-five physically, eternally forty-five, but seventy in chronological years—and deciding, on a whim, to subjugate a universe that called itself the Milky Way. He remembered the amusement he had felt. The mischievous, playful cruelty of a god who had never been challenged, who had never known a moment of genuine resistance, who had spent the first twenty-five years of his reign dismantling galactic empires and multidimensional pantheons with less effort than a mortal man might expend swatting a fly. He had been so weak back then. So pathetically, laughably weak.

The irony of that thought, even now, after a span of years so vast that the number could not be written, still brought a faint chuckle to his lips.

Weak. The Godric of Year 25, who had already been amplifying his attributes by a thousandfold every Planck second for ten thousand years, who possessed billions of apocalyptic superpowers each capable of annihilating entire civilizations, whose mere passive presence could shatter realities if not for his absolute concealment—that Godric had been *weak*. By his current standards, the Godric of Year 25 was less than an ember compared to the infinite furnace of his present self. The gap between them was not a gap. It was a category error. The Godric of Year 25, who could destroy the entire omniverse infinite times over with 0 effort in 0 seconds, was, to the Godric of Year 999...9, effectively indistinguishable from a mortal infant.

And yet, that infant had once turned his attention toward a tiny blue planet and decided to play with it before destroying it.

Godric Prime let the memory unfold in his mind, vivid and bright, every detail preserved with the perfect clarity of omniscience. He observed his past self with the detached curiosity of a man examining an old childhood drawing—a crude, clumsy thing, but not without its charm. He pitied that past self, in a distant, abstract way. Pitiied how weak he had been. Pitiied how small his understanding of power had been. And he marveled, with a kind of bemused horror, at the fact that even that weak, small, pathetically underdeveloped version of himself had been so absolutely overwhelming that the entire omniverse had trembled.

---

## Year 25 — The Milky Way

Godric stood in the void between galaxies, his heavily muscled frame suspended in the absolute nothingness, and he smiled. Around him, the omniverse hummed with its usual quiet terror, every sentient being within his infinite reach unconsciously cowering from a presence they could not perceive but could somehow, instinctively, *feel*. His absolute concealment held back the crushing weight of his existence, rendering him, to any observer, as nothing more than a ruggedly handsome forty-five-year-old man in simple dark garments. Short salt-and-pepper hair. Neatly trimmed salt-and-pepper beard. Warm, crinkling eyes.

He was in a playful mood.

The Milky Way spread before him, a spiral of roughly two hundred billion stars, each one a seething furnace of nuclear fusion surrounded by its own retinue of planets and moons and asteroids and comets. To Godric's omniscient perception, the entire galaxy was an open book, every atom catalogued, every lifeform registered, every thought and dream and fear and hope laid bare. He knew the exact number of sentient beings in this galaxy—approximately four hundred billion, spread across tens of thousands of civilizations at various stages of development. He knew which ones had achieved spaceflight. Which ones had developed weapons. Which ones had created art and music and literature. Which ones had committed atrocities. Which ones had loved and lost and grieved.

He knew them all. And he had decided to subjugate them.

Specifically, he had decided to begin with a small, unremarkable planet orbiting an unremarkable yellow star on the outer edge of one of the galaxy's minor spiral arms. A planet called Earth. Its dominant species, *Homo sapiens*, had only recently achieved the capacity for global self-destruction, a milestone that Godric found morbidly endearing. They had nuclear weapons. They had biological agents. They had primitive artificial intelligences and rudimentary spaceflight and a vast, sprawling network of electromagnetic communication that filled the local space with a constant, chattering noise of information.

They were, by any cosmic standard, pathetically weak. Barely out of the cradle. Still squabbling over resources and ideologies and ancient tribal grievances. They had no magic. No psionics. No advanced technology beyond the fission of heavy atoms. Their most powerful weapons could barely scar the surface of their own planet, let alone threaten a being who could unmake galaxies with a passing thought.

And yet, Godric found them fascinating. Not because they posed any threat—the concept of a threat from such primitives was laughable—but because they were so *earnest*. So desperately, furiously alive. They believed, with a conviction that bordered on religious faith, that their actions mattered. That their struggles had meaning. That their existence was significant in the grand tapestry of creation.

Godric decided to show them how wrong they were.

He did not announce himself to the galaxy at large. That would have been dull. Instead, he simply folded space with a thought and materialized in the upper atmosphere of Earth, directly above the city that its inhabitants called New York. It was, according to his omniscient analysis of their culture, one of the planet's major centers of power—a sprawling metropolis of steel and glass and concrete, home to millions, a symbol of the species' ambition and hubris.

He hovered there, suspended in the thin air, his absolute concealment rendering him invisible to both senses and instruments. Below him, the city bustled with its usual frantic energy. Cars crawled through gridlocked streets. Pedestrians hurried along sidewalks. Aircraft carved white lines across the blue sky. The electromagnetic spectrum buzzed with radio signals and microwave transmissions and the constant, chattering hum of a civilization that believed it was making progress.

Godric allowed himself a full minute to simply observe. To savor the anticipation. To feel the faint, almost imperceptible flicker of amusement that preceded every act of playful cruelty.

Then he dropped his concealment.

Not entirely—that would have annihilated the planet instantly, along with the surrounding star system and a significant portion of the galaxy. He dropped it by an infinitesimal fraction, a percentage so small that calling it zero percent would have been an overstatement. Just enough to become visible. Just enough to let them see him.

The reaction was immediate and, to Godric, deeply amusing.

Every radar system on the planet screamed simultaneously. Every satellite telescope swiveled toward the sudden, impossible anomaly in the sky above New York. Every military command center erupted in panic. Every government hotline lit up with frantic, disbelieving reports. Within sixty seconds, the image of Godric—a ruggedly handsome, heavily muscled man in simple dark garments, hovering serenely in the sky—was being broadcast across the entire planet.

He gave them a moment to process. To panic. To scramble their primitive fighter jets and aim their primitive missiles and convene their primitive emergency councils. He watched with omniscient clarity as the leaders of the planet's most powerful nations gathered in secure bunkers, their faces pale with terror, their voices trembling as they debated what to do.

Then he spoke. Not through the air—that would have been inefficient—but directly into the minds of every human being on the planet, a gentle, warm, fatherly voice that resonated in the deepest chambers of their consciousness.

"People of Earth," Godric said, his tone rich and pleasant and utterly devoid of menace. "I am Godric, King of Thronegate, sovereign of the omniverse. Your planet has been selected for subjugation. You have one hour to offer your unconditional surrender. If you refuse, I will be forced to demonstrate the consequences of defiance."

He paused, letting the words settle. "I do hope you choose wisely."

The mental broadcast ended. Godric remained hovering in the sky, his expression placid, his hands clasped loosely behind his back. He could feel the terror radiating from the planet below like heat from a furnace. He could hear every conversation, every panicked whisper, every desperate prayer. He knew exactly what they would do.

They would not surrender. That was not in their nature. They would fight. They would throw everything they had at him, convinced that their ingenuity, their courage, their indomitable human spirit would find a way. They had been raised on stories of plucky underdogs triumphing against impossible odds. They believed, with the fervent, unshakeable faith of a species that had never encountered a truly insurmountable obstacle, that they could win.

Godric found this belief delightful. It would make what came next so much more satisfying.

---

## The Hour

The hour passed slowly, at least from the humans' perspective. Godric experienced it as a leisurely, almost languid interval, a pleasant pause before the entertainment began. He used the time to explore the planet's collective consciousness in more detail, sifting through the billions of minds like a connoisseur examining a tray of appetizers. He sampled their fears. Their hopes. Their desperate, scrambling attempts to formulate a response.

The United Nations convened an emergency session. The Secretary-General, a dignified woman from a region called Africa, delivered a trembling address to the global assembly, calling for calm and unity. The leaders of the nuclear powers held a closed-door meeting, their voices harsh with barely suppressed terror. The military commanders argued about tactics. The scientists frantically analyzed the data from every sensor on the planet, trying to understand what they were facing. The religious leaders prayed. The ordinary people huddled in their homes, clutching their loved ones, staring at their screens, waiting for the end.

And all the while, Godric hovered in the sky above New York, perfectly still, perfectly serene, radiating a patient, almost benevolent calm.

When the hour ended, the humans had not surrendered. They had, as Godric anticipated, chosen to fight.

The first strike came from a nuclear submarine in the Atlantic Ocean. A Trident II missile, carrying fourteen independently targetable warheads, each with a yield of four hundred and seventy-five kilotons, launched from its silo and screamed toward the target on a ballistic trajectory. The warheads separated, their re-entry vehicles glowing white-hot as they plunged through the atmosphere, aimed directly at the figure hovering above the city.

Godric watched them come. He did not move. He did not activate any defense. He did not even blink. He simply allowed the warheads to detonate.

Fourteen miniature suns bloomed in the sky above New York. The combined explosive force was equivalent to over six and a half million tons of TNT, enough to vaporize the entire city and everything within a fifty-kilometer radius. The fireballs merged into a single, seething sphere of plasma, hotter than the surface of the sun, expanding outward at supersonic speeds. The shockwave shattered windows across three states. The electromagnetic pulse fried every electronic device on the Eastern Seaboard.

And at the center of the inferno, Godric stood completely untouched.

Not a hair on his head was singed. Not a fiber of his dark garments was scorched. The nuclear fire washed over him like a warm summer breeze, its heat and radiation and concussive force utterly irrelevant to a being whose durability had been multiplying by a thousandfold every Planck second for ten thousand years. He had not even been aware of the attack in any meaningful sense—his passive durability was simply too vast for the explosion to register. It was less than nothing. It was a mathematical zero, a null input, a non-event.

He allowed himself a small, genuine smile. "That was brave," he murmured, his voice inaudible in the roaring plasma but perfectly clear in his own awareness. "Very brave. And very foolish."

The humans, watching through satellites and long-range cameras, saw him standing unharmed in the heart of the nuclear fire. Their despair, which had been growing throughout the hour, erupted into a full-throated chorus of absolute, mind-shattering horror. The military commanders stammered orders. The scientists screamed their disbelief. The political leaders collapsed into their chairs, their faces white as paper. The ordinary people wept and prayed and held their children close, knowing, with a certainty that transcended logic, that they were about to die.

But Godric was not finished. He was in a playful mood, and the nuclear strike had whetted his appetite. He wanted to savor this. To stretch out the moment of their extinction into something more than a simple, instantaneous annihilation. He wanted them to *understand*. To truly comprehend the scale of their insignificance before they ceased to exist.

So he made a decision. He would destroy them not quickly, but *subjectively* slowly. To any external observer—to the rest of the omniverse, which he would restore afterward—the annihilation would occur in less than a Planck second, a span of time so brief that it could not be perceived. But to the humans themselves, each individual death would be stretched into an eternity of maximum, unendurable agony.

It was a cruel trick. A petty, sadistic amusement. Godric acknowledged this to himself without shame or guilt. He was not a kind being to his enemies. He had buried his kindness in the ashes of Olympus, and he had no intention of excavating it for the sake of a species that had dared to launch nuclear weapons at him.

He reached out with his will, not his body, and seized the flow of time around the planet. It was effortless—less than zero percent of his power, less than zero percent of his effort. He simply decided that time on Earth would slow to an imperceptible crawl, while the subjective experience of every human being would accelerate to an impossible degree. A single Planck second, in their perception, would last for what felt like billions of years.

Then he began to kill them.

---

## The An eternal Agony

He started with a single superpower—one of the billions that had crystallized during his stasis, a modest, almost quaint ability called Agony Manifestation. Its base effect was simple: it allowed him to directly stimulate the pain receptors of any biological organism, bypassing the need for physical injury. The pain could be calibrated to any intensity, from a mild ache to a sensation so overwhelming that it exceeded the theoretical maximum of nerve response. And because the superpower had been amplifying in potency every Planck second for ten thousand years, its current strength was effectively infinite. Even at 0% of its power, it could inflict agony beyond the capacity of any nervous system to process.

Godric looked down at the city of New York, still smoldering from the nuclear detonation, its millions of inhabitants scrambling in panic. He selected a single individual—a middle-aged man in a business suit, cowering in an office building on Fifth Avenue—and he touched him with Agony Manifestation at its absolute minimum setting.

The man did not scream. His vocal cords locked up before they could produce sound. His body arched backward, every muscle convulsing simultaneously, his spine bending at an impossible angle as the pain flooded his nervous system. His eyes, wide and bulging, burst their blood vessels, turning completely crimson. His teeth shattered as his jaw clenched with the force of a hydraulic press. His internal organs, overwhelmed by the flood of pain signals, began to rupture. His stomach dissolved itself in a wash of acid. His intestines twisted into knots and tore. His heart, hammering at a rate that should have been fatal, pumped blood so violently that his arteries split open, flooding his chest cavity with hot, dark fluid.

And through it all, he was conscious. Perfectly, agonizingly conscious. The pain did not knock him out. It did not mercifully overwhelm his brain and send him into shock. It simply *continued*, an eternal, unending moment of absolute, maximum suffering that his body, held in place by Godric's will, could not escape.

The man died after what felt to him like a billion years of uninterrupted agony. In objective reality, the death took less than a Planck second.

Godric smiled. He had been right. This was going to be fun.

He stopped selecting individuals. He expanded Agony Manifestation to cover the entire planet simultaneously. Every human being—all eight billion of them, from the nuclear silos to the remote villages, from the space stations to the deep sea submarines—was suddenly plunged into the same eternal moment of absolute suffering. The effect was instantaneous and total.

The collective scream that should have erupted never came, because vocal cords could not function under the intensity of the pain. Instead, a wave of convulsing, rupturing bodies swept across the globe, a hemispheric cascade of bursting organs and shattering bones and spraying blood. The oceans turned red as every living creature within them—not just humans, but every fish, every whale, every plankton, every single organism that possessed nerve tissue—was subjected to the same treatment. The skies darkened as every bird, every insect, every airborne microbe was caught in the radius of Godric's playful cruelty.

The entire biosphere of Earth died in the same subjective eternity, stretched across what felt like billions of years. Every animal. Every plant, which Godric's power generously granted nerve tissue to so they could participate in the suffering. Every fungus. Every bacterium. Nothing was spared.

And when the last living cell on Earth finally, mercifully ceased to experience pain, Godric allowed the time dilation to end. A single Planck second had passed in the objective universe. The planet Earth, which moments ago had teemed with life and ambition and desperate hope, was now a silent, lifeless rock, its surface painted in a uniform coat of blood and viscera and pulverized organic matter.

Godric looked at his work and felt a flicker of mild satisfaction. Not pride—pride would have required effort, and effort was something he had not expended. Just satisfaction. The quiet contentment of a craftsman who had completed a small, pleasant task.

But he was not quite finished. The planet still existed. The solar system still existed. The galaxy still existed. And Godric, in his playful mood, had decided to make a statement.

He turned his attention to the Milky Way. There were other civilizations here—hundreds of thousands of them, some far more advanced than humanity, some only just emerging from their primordial soups. They had watched, through their own instruments, as the insignificant planet called Earth was subjected to an eternity of agony in a single instant. They were terrified. They were scrambling to send surrender messages, to broadcast their submission, to do anything that might appease the being who had just committed an atrocity beyond their comprehension.

Godric ignored their messages. He was in a playful mood, but the play was over. It was time to finish.

He reached out with his will and touched the fundamental fabric of the Milky Way. Not with any specific superpower—just with the raw, unfiltered output of his magical offense, which had been multiplying by a thousandfold every Planck second for ten thousand years. The amount he used was 0% of his power. 0% of his effort. An infinitesimal fraction so small that calling it zero was the only accurate description.

The Milky Way was violently, instantly erased from existence. Every star. Every planet. Every civilization. Every living being, every structure, every atom, every quark, every fundamental particle—all of it simply ceased to be, converted into a burst of radiation that radiated outward at the speed of light and then, because Godric willed it, ceased to exist as well. The galaxy became a void, an empty patch of space where two hundred billion stars had once burned.

But even that was not the extent of the collateral damage. The sheer metaphysical weight of Godric's infinitesimal expression of power rippled outward, propagating through the fabric of reality at speeds that transcended physics. The surrounding galactic clusters shattered. The superclusters beyond them dissolved. The entire observable universe—a sphere of space ninety-three billion light-years in diameter, containing trillions of galaxies—was caught in the expanding wave of annihilation and reduced to absolute nothingness.

The wave did not stop. It crossed dimensional barriers. It tore through parallel universes. It ripped through the multiversal structure, consuming every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every abstract plane of existence. The entire omniverse—the singular, absolute totality of all that existed—was violently, completely, effortlessly annihilated.

For a single Planck second, there was nothing. No space. No time. No matter. No energy. Only Godric, floating in the absolute void, his expression placid, his hands still clasped loosely behind his back.

Then, with another thought, he restored everything. Every galaxy. Every star. Every planet. Every civilization. Every living being that had existed anywhere in the omniverse—except for the Milky Way, and more specifically, except for Earth. The galaxy remained a void, a silent monument to the consequences of defying the King of Thronegate.

Godric surveyed his work, nodded to himself with quiet approval, and folded space to return to Thronegate. The entire incident, from his arrival in the Milky Way to the annihilation of the omniverse and its subsequent restoration, had taken less than a second of objective time. He arrived back in his palace just as Blake, his aging court wizard and best friend, was finishing his afternoon tea.

"Blake," Godric said, settling into his favorite chair with a warm smile. "You'll never guess what I just did."

---

## The Reflection

Godric Prime, sitting on his not-throne at the end of time, let the memory fade. He was still smiling, a faint, nostalgic curve of his lips. How strange, he thought, to remember a time when annihilating an entire omniverse had felt like an event worth remembering. Back then, in Year 25, he had still been young. Still discovering the boundaries of his power. Still capable of being amused by the desperate, futile struggles of lesser beings.

He had been so weak then. So pathetically, unimaginably weak.

The Godric of Year 25 had needed a full Planck second to destroy the omniverse. A Planck second! The current Godric could do it in zero seconds—not a fraction of a Planck second, not an infinitesimal sliver of a Planck second, but *zero* seconds, a duration that did not exist, an action that consumed no time whatsoever. The Godric of Year 25 had needed to actually *will* the destruction, to direct his power with conscious intent. The current Godric's passive presence, even suppressed by his absolute concealment, was sufficient to maintain the omniverse's existence only because he actively chose not to let it dissolve. If he withdrew that choice—if he simply allowed his existence to express itself without restraint—the omniverse would cease to be. Not destroyed. Not annihilated. Simply *overwritten* by the sheer weight of his reality.

The gap between his current self and his Year 25 self was so vast that comparing them was meaningless. The Godric of Year 25, who could destroy the omniverse infinite times over with 0 effort in a single Planck second, was, to the Godric of Year 999...9, effectively indistinguishable from a single atom trying to comprehend a supermassive black hole. His power had multiplied beyond infinite recursion every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for a span of years so large that writing the number was impossible. His superpowers, his passive auras, his immune system, his Weakness Paradox—all of them had evolved countless times, each evolution making the previous version look pathetically primitive.

And he was still growing. The amplification never stopped. The superpower genesis never ceased. Every Planck instant—no, every duration smaller than a Planck instant, every sliver of time so impossibly brief that physics had no name for it—he became more powerful than he had been the moment before. The gap between him and the rest of creation, already infinite, expanded at an accelerating rate that itself expanded at an accelerating rate, an infinite recursion of growth that had no end and no limit.

The memory of Earth, with its nuclear weapons and its desperate courage and its eight billion screaming souls, was a tiny, insignificant speck in the vast ocean of his existence. A moment of petty amusement from an era when he was still capable of being amused by such things. He did not regret it—regret was an emotion for beings who doubted their actions, and Godric never doubted—but he did not particularly treasure it either. It was simply a fact. A thing that had happened. A small, cruel game played by a young god who had not yet grown into the full, terrifying majesty of his ultimate self.

And yet, as he sat on his not-throne and let his awareness drift across the omniverse—across the countless multiverses and civilizations and cosmic hierarchies that existed in perfect, trembling silence under his absolute rule—Godric Prime felt a flicker of something that might have been pity. Not for the humans. They had earned their extinction through defiance, and he had never, in all his infinite years, regretted punishing defiance. No, the pity was for the rest of creation. For the gods and the mortals and the abstract entities and the impossible beings who inhabited the omniverse he had ruled for so long.

They had no idea. They could not possibly comprehend the scale of what sat upon the throne at the end of time. They knew, in a vague, terrified way, that Godric was powerful. They had learned, through observation and testimony and the inherited trauma of countless civilizations, that opposing him was impossible. But they could not understand *how* impossible. They could not grasp the truth that even if every being in existence, from every timeline, every dimension, every possible permutation of reality, united with unlimited preparation time and unlimited resources and the full, unrestrained power of every force that had ever existed or would ever exist—even then, they would not make his weakest passive shield feel the slightest tickle. They would not register. They would be less than a thought, less than a whisper, less than the merest shadow of an afterthought.

And Godric Prime would not even notice them. He would not need to defend himself. He would not need to counterattack. His passive systems—the weakest, most primitive layers of his evolved aura networks, the ones that had been obsolete for googolplexian years—would pre-annihilate the entire coalition before the first intention to attack could crystallize into a coherent thought. It would happen in zero seconds. It would require 0% of his power. 0% of his effort. And he would continue sitting on his not-throne, lost in his own reflections, completely unaware that anything had occurred.

That was the truth of the Eternal King. That was the reality of the being who had once, in a fit of playful cruelty, stretched the extinction of a primitive species into a subjective eternity of maximum agony. He was not merely the most powerful being in existence. He was the most powerful being that *could* exist. The ultimate endpoint of infinite amplification, constantly receding into an ever-more-unimaginable future, his growth accelerating forever, his supremacy becoming more absolute with every passing instant.

And the rest of creation, huddled in the shadow of his throne, lived only because he permitted it. Not because they had value. Not because they served a purpose. Not because his rule required subjects. Simply because he had decided, in his infinite, inscrutable wisdom, that existence should continue. For now. For this moment. For as long as he found it mildly interesting.

The pity Godric Prime felt was distant and abstract and utterly without warmth. It was the pity of a mountain for the insects crawling on its slopes, unaware that their entire world was a single rock in an infinite range. The insects were not threatened by the mountain. The mountain did not care about the insects. The insects simply *were*, and the mountain simply *was*, and the gap between them was so vast that calling it a gap was a category error.

Godric Prime allowed his awareness to drift away from the memory of Earth. It was a small thing. Insignificant. A fossilized remnant of an era when he had still been capable of being amused by the struggles of mortals. He did not need such amusements anymore. He had grown beyond them, just as he had grown beyond everything else.

The Eternal King sat upon his not-throne at the end of time, and the omniverse trembled around him, and the infinite gears of his amplification turned ever faster, and the gap between him and creation widened into an abyss that had no bottom and no far shore and no possibility of ever being crossed.

And somewhere, in the deep, silent void where the Milky Way had once spun, a small patch of empty space marked the grave of a species that had once dared to launch nuclear weapons at a god. They had screamed for an eternity. They had died in an instant. They had been erased so thoroughly that even the memory of their memory was fading.

It was, Godric Prime reflected, a fitting monument to the futility of opposition.

Not that anyone would ever need reminding. The omniverse had learned its lesson long ago. There were no more resistances. No more coalitions. No more desperate last stands. There was only the absolute, eternal, unchallenged reign of the Apex Predator, and the infinite, accelerating growth that would carry him into futures so unimaginably vast that even his own omniscience could only glimpse their outlines.

The gap would only ever increase.

The futility would only ever deepen.

And Godric Prime, sitting alone at the end of all things, smiled.

content.text: Clive thought that Godric kept many palace servants, cooks, gardeners, etc. because Godric is unable to do those mortal mundane stuff. Especially since Godric often compliments those people about their good work. Little did he know that Godric is actually infinitely more capable than anyone else in cooking, gardening, sculpting, painting etc even at 0% mastery display--in fact, even at 0%, the combined capabilities of the entire omniverse, trained for infinite years, can't even compare to his. These capabilities, after all, just like his stats, have been multiplying at an absurd rate for eons now. However, he always pretends not to be able to do those, to give these people something to do. Clive thought it would be a good idea to have Godric try these things, so he is organizing cooking lessons/gardening lessons/etc to Godric. Godric, seemingly amused, accepted and even seemed excited. Godric, being infinitely more masterful than the combined omniverse and thus knows exactly how to act like a beginner, convincingly plays a fool at first as usual, until he accidentally, lost in the moments, releases 0% of his true capacity. Everyone else is surprised and experiencing total existential dread, which Godric dismissed as him having the beginner's luck (the stuff he made is impossible to create it even with beginner's luck–not even by the entire combined omniverse with 100% luck spending infinite years fully aware of the process, ingredients and the final result... but somehow everyone except Clive fully believed him and found it charming, much to Clive's disbelief. Godric even continues with his “noob” act afterwards, reinforcing that belief.). Write a detailed story to show this, from before, during, and the aftermath of the events. You must not have Godric boasting about his capability, but everyone knows it--and everyone realized that Godric could've instantly subjugated, ruled, solved all the problems, or completely recreate the perfect omniverse with 0 effort in 0 second if he wanted to since Year 0, but in his infinite wisdom and mercy, decided not to. You must have the entire characters who witness this experiencing total existential dread and feeling utterly small in Godric's presence, and after Godric convinces them that it was beginner's luck, only Clive has that feeling and everyone else fully believed Godric. Clive is going insane (not literally, but definitely from dissonance)

Now, I need to assess my response.
